claims.claim_text: The module opens with a specific November 2023 Black Friday AKS outage at an unnamed online retailer.
claims.anchor_text: In November 2023, an online retailer running on AKS experienced a catastrophic failure during their Black Friday sale.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common AKS production failure mode is hitting a storage bottleneck during a major traffic surge.
claims.rationale: This is a named-by-time incident anecdote with no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The outage involved a 15x traffic spike exhausting disk IOPS and queuing writes.
claims.anchor_text: When traffic spiked to 15x normal levels, the disk IOPS ceiling was hit and writes started queuing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When traffic spikes sharply, a disk IOPS ceiling can cause writes to queue.
claims.rationale: The precise multiplier is uncited and should be removed while preserving the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The on-call engineer scaled from 6 to 30 replicas, worsening contention for one disk's IOPS budget.
claims.anchor_text: Without visibility into the real bottleneck, the on-call engineer scaled the deployment from 6 to 30 replicas, which made things dramatically worse: 30 pods now competed for the same disk's IOPS budget.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Without visibility into the real bottleneck, scaling the deployment can make the problem worse by increasing contention for the same storage resource.
claims.rationale: The specific replica counts are part of an unsourced incident narrative.

claims.claim_text: The order pipeline froze for 90 minutes and caused $4.2 million in lost revenue.
claims.anchor_text: The queue grew, timeouts cascaded, and the entire order pipeline froze for 90 minutes during peak sales hours. Post-incident analysis estimated $4.2 million in lost revenue.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The queue grew, timeouts cascaded, and the order pipeline stalled during a peak-sales window, creating significant business impact.
claims.rationale: These are fake-precise outage and revenue figures without a citable source.

claims.claim_text: The retailer's remediation was Ultra Disks, disk I/O Grafana metrics, and KEDA scaling on queue depth.
claims.anchor_text: The fix for the retailer was straightforward: migrate to Ultra Disks with provisioned IOPS, add disk I/O metrics to their Grafana dashboards, and implement KEDA-based scaling that responded to queue depth rather than CPU utilization.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A practical remediation is to provision storage for the required IOPS, add disk I/O observability, and scale workers on a workload signal such as queue depth instead of CPU alone.
claims.rationale: This is still part of the unsourced retailer story and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: Azure Files provides shared storage for AKS workloads with `ReadWriteMany` access across pods on different nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Files: Shared Storage for Multi-Pod Access
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents Azure Files for AKS as RWX shared storage and Kubernetes defines RWX semantics.

claims.claim_text: Azure Shared Disks are for clustered applications, require coordination, and do not provide a managed shared filesystem.
claims.span_hint: section: Shared Disks for High Availability
claims.rationale: Microsoft states that shared disks are for clustered applications and do not natively provide an SMB/NFS-style managed filesystem.

claims.claim_text: Mounting a shared Azure disk with `ext4` or `xfs` from multiple Linux nodes will immediately corrupt data.
claims.span_hint: section: Shared Disks for High Availability
claims.anchor_text: If you merely mount a shared disk using `ext4` or `xfs` from multiple Linux nodes, the kernel's in-memory caching will immediately corrupt your data.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Do not mount a shared block disk from multiple nodes unless the application or filesystem is explicitly designed for coordinated multi-writer access.
claims.rationale: The exact corruption wording is stronger and more specific than the cited shared-disk guidance supports, so it should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: By default, Container Insights captures every line from every container's standard output.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Control for Container Insights
claims.anchor_text: By default, it captures every single line of text from every container's standard output.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By default, Container insights can ingest a large volume of stdout and stderr logs, so noisy workloads should be filtered to control Log Analytics cost.
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents broad default log collection, but not this absolute phrasing.

claims.claim_text: Container Insights supports ConfigMap-based filtering such as excluding noisy namespaces and disabling environment-variable collection.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Control for Container Insights
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents `container-azm-ms-agentconfig`, namespace exclusions, and `env_var.enabled` settings.

claims.claim_text: Azure Monitor managed service for Prometheus is a fully managed Prometheus-compatible service with up to 18 months of retention at no additional storage cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Prometheus and Grafana: Cloud-Native Monitoring
claims.rationale: Microsoft explicitly documents the managed service behavior and retention policy.

claims.claim_text: Azure Managed Grafana integrates with Microsoft Entra ID and Azure RBAC.
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents Entra-based authentication and Azure RBAC role assignments for Azure Managed Grafana.

claims.claim_text: Adding standard Prometheus pod annotations is enough for Azure Managed Prometheus to automatically scrape application metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: Custom Prometheus Metrics from Your Application
claims.anchor_text: The Managed Prometheus agent will automatically discover these endpoints and begin scraping.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Azure Managed Prometheus can scrape application metrics once you configure scraping for annotated pods or use PodMonitor or ServiceMonitor resources.
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents annotation-based scraping as a configured option rather than an automatic default for all annotated pods.

claims.claim_text: KEDA supports scale-to-zero on AKS.
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS KEDA documentation explicitly calls out scale-to-zero as a capability.

claims.claim_text: KEDA provides over 60 custom scalers.
claims.rationale: This appears real in the upstream KEDA docs, but `keda.sh` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The cluster autoscaler watches unschedulable pods and scales node pools up or down accordingly.
claims.span_hint: section: Combining KEDA with Cluster Autoscaler
claims.rationale: Microsoft explicitly documents AKS cluster autoscaler behavior around unschedulable pods and node scale-down.

claims.claim_text: AKS Spot node pools are labeled and tainted with `kubernetes.azure.com/scalesetpriority=spot`, requiring corresponding tolerations for scheduling.
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents the Spot node pool label, taint, and scheduling requirements.

claims.claim_text: A 64 GiB Ultra Disk can be provisioned for 50,000 IOPS.
claims.anchor_text: Ultra Disk lets you provision 50,000 IOPS on a 64 GB disk.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Ultra Disk and Premium SSD v2 let you provision higher performance more independently than Premium SSD v1, but the exact IOPS limits still depend on the disk type and size.
claims.rationale: Current Microsoft disk performance documentation does not support this exact number for a 64 GiB Ultra Disk.

claims.claim_text: The standard HPA cannot scale to zero and requires at least one replica.
claims.anchor_text: KEDA can scale to zero replicas, which the standard HPA cannot do.** The HPA requires a minimum of 1 replica.
claims.suggested_rewrite: KEDA is commonly used when you need reliable scale-to-zero behavior for event-driven workloads; plain HPA does not typically provide that in standard configurations.
claims.rationale: The blanket statement is too strong given Kubernetes' `HPAScaleToZero` feature gate history and should be qualified.

claims.claim_text: A single Azure Files NFS connection tops out around 300-400 MB/s and `nconnect=4` effectively quadruples throughput.
claims.anchor_text: A single NFS connection typically tops out at 300-400 MB/s due to TCP window limitations. Setting `nconnect=4` in your StorageClass mount options opens 4 parallel TCP connections per mount, effectively quadrupling throughput.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Using `nconnect=4` is the current Azure Files recommendation for NFS and can materially improve throughput, but the exact gain depends on the client, workload, and share configuration.
claims.rationale: Microsoft recommends `nconnect=4` and reports variable gains, but this exact throughput ceiling and guaranteed 4x result are too specific.

further_reading.title: Azure Disk CSI Driver on AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for AKS disk provisioning, topology-aware binding, and Azure Disk CSI behavior.

further_reading.title: Monitor AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Microsoft's overview for AKS observability, including Container insights, managed Prometheus, and Managed Grafana.

further_reading.title: KEDA on AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains what the AKS KEDA add-on supports and when to use event-driven autoscaling instead of basic HPA patterns.

notes: Code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz content, and lab solution steps were excluded per the task rules. I also left table cells out of the seed; the prose around them already captures the highest-risk citation gaps, but the storage and KEDA tables may warrant a separate pass if your validator treats factual table rows as citation-bearing content.
